name: User Profile Manager
scenario: You're building the backend user-profile system for a social platform (think LinkedIn, Twitter, or a SaaS product). User profiles have complex state: read-only fields (userId, registrationDate), modifiable fields with rules (email must match a pattern, age must be 13-120), computed fields (account-age in days), and sensitive fields (password — never readable). This is real production-style backend design — every signup form on the internet has logic like this behind it.
description: Build a `UserProfile` class with full encapsulation: private fields, validating setters where appropriate, read-only fields where modification isn't allowed, computed properties, and security-aware design (password is set-only, never gettable). Then build a `UserDirectory` class that manages multiple users.

core.description: Fields: userId (int, final), username (String, final), email (String), age (int), passwordHash (String). registrationYear (int, final).

Constructor takes userId, username, email, age, password, registrationYear. Validates: userId > 0, username non-empty, email contains '@', age 13-120. Password stored as 'hash' (use reverse for this project).
Getters: getUserId, getUsername, getEmail, getAge, getRegistrationYear. NO getter for password!
Setters: setEmail (validates @ present), setAge (validates 13-120). NO setters for userId, username, registrationYear, password.
`changePassword(String old, String new)` — must provide correct old password to set new. Returns boolean.
`verifyPassword(String attempt)` — returns true if matches. (Tests can be authenticated this way.)
main() demonstrates: creating valid user, attempting invalid creation, getters, valid email update, invalid age update, password change (success and failure), account age

bonus.description: Add `UserDirectory` class with private User[] field, addUser(UserProfile), findByUsername(String) returning user or null
Add `getEmailDomain()` to UserProfile — extracts domain part of email (after @)
Add `isAdult()` to UserProfile — boolean getter, true if age >= 18
Add UserDirectory.countByEmailDomain(String domain) — counts users with email at that domain

data_to_use:
Test scenario in main():

1. Create user:
   - userId: 101
   - username: 'aisha_k'
   - email: 'aisha@gmail.com'
   - age: 28
   - password: 'mypass123'
   - registrationYear: 2020

2. Try invalid creations (each in try/catch):
   - userId: -1 → rejected
   - email: 'no-at-sign' → rejected
   - age: 150 → rejected

3. Operations:
   - Print getters
   - setEmail('aisha.k@yahoo.com') → success
   - setAge(200) → rejected
   - verifyPassword('mypass123') → true
   - verifyPassword('wrong') → false
   - changePassword('wrong', 'new123') → false (rejected)
   - changePassword('mypass123', 'new123') → true
   - verifyPassword('new123') → true
   - getAccountAgeYears(2024) → 4
example_output:
===================================
      USER PROFILE MANAGER
===================================
Created user: aisha_k
User ID: 101 | Email: aisha@gmail.com | Age: 28

Validation tests:
Rejected: User ID must be positive
Rejected: Email must contain @
Rejected: Age must be between 13 and 120

After email update: aisha.k@yahoo.com
Rejected age update: Age must be between 13 and 120

Password verification:
verify 'mypass123': true
verify 'wrong': false

Password change:
With wrong old: false
With correct old: true
verify new password: true

Account age in 2024: 4 years
===================================
ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 AI hints. The most important design decisions: (1) which fields get setters and which don't, (2) password NEVER has a getter, (3) changePassword requires the old password to be verified before allowing change. These are real security patterns.
submission_instructions: Save as ProfileManager.java. Run it. Push to GitHub — this is REAL user management backend logic. Many real signup systems work exactly like this.
tips_for_success: Fields that should NEVER change after creation: use private final and NO setter (userId, username, registrationYear)
Fields that allow controlled change: private + validating setter (email, age)
Sensitive fields: private, NO getter, only verify/change methods (password)
Use the 'reverse string' trick as your hash function — real apps use SHA-256
changePassword: first verify old password matches, then 'hash' and store new
Computed properties don't need a field — just compute in the getter
For bonus UserDirectory: similar to Library pattern from 4.2
concepts_reinforced: Final fields for true immutability of value
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this:
User profile systems are PERFECT for teaching encapsulation because they have a mix of fields with different access needs. Real-world authentication systems do EXACTLY what you're doing here:
- Permanent identifiers (userId) can't be changed without breaking everything
- Mutable profile data (email, age) needs validation on every change
- Sensitive data (password) is set-only — you can verify or change it, but never see it
- Computed values (account age) prevent stale data

If you understand this project, you understand the architecture of every signup/profile system in production. Companies like Razorpay, Stripe, and HDFC NetBanking have systems built on these same principles — just with more sophisticated hashing (bcrypt, scrypt, argon2) and additional security layers.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Replace 'reverse' hash with a real SHA-256 implementation (java.security.MessageDigest)
Add password strength validation (length, complexity) using regex
Add a 'lastLoginDate' field with proper Date handling
Implement profile picture URL with validation (must be http/https)
Add audit log: track every email change with timestamp
Convert to use the Builder pattern for the complex constructor
